comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q570140]] Wikidata name: Taika Reform [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{ill|Kan'ichi Asakawa|en|Kan'ichi Asakawa|WD=Q3138143|lt=Asakawa, Kan'ichi}}. (1903). [https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

==Further reading== * {{ill|Kan'ichi Asakawa|en|Kan'ichi Asakawa|WD=Q3138143|lt=Asakawa, Kan'ichi}}. (1903). [https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform|1=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies|1=Chinese philosophy}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system|1=Written Chinese}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature|1=Chinese literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion|1=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture|1=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural|1=Culture of Japan}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe|1=Emperor Tenji}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law|1=Law of Japan}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code|1=Taihō Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''|1=Daijō-daijin}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces|1=Provinces of Japan}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an|1=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{ill|Kan'ichi Asakawa|en|Kan'ichi Asakawa|WD=Q3138143|lt=Asakawa, Kan'ichi|1=Kan'ichi Asakawa}}. (1903). [https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha|1=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform|1=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies|1=Chinese philosophy}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system|1=Written Chinese}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature|1=Chinese literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion|1=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture|1=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural|1=Culture of Japan}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe|1=Emperor Tenji}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident|1=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro|1=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko|1=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China|1=Sui dynasty}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China|1=Japanese missions to Imperial China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control|1=Sinicization}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law|1=Law of Japan}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code|1=Taihō Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''|1=Daijō-daijin}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces|1=Provinces of Japan}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an|1=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

[https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha|1=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform|1=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies|1=Chinese philosophy}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system|1=Written Chinese}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature|1=Chinese literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion|1=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture|1=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural|1=Culture of Japan}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe|1=Emperor Tenji}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident|1=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro|1=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko|1=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China|1=Sui dynasty}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China|1=Japanese missions to Imperial China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control|1=Sinicization}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law|1=Law of Japan}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code|1=Taihō Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''|1=Daijō-daijin}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces|1=Provinces of Japan}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an|1=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

[https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha|1=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org]

реформе]][[sv:Taikareformerna]][[th:การปฏิรูปปีไทกะ]][[tr:Taika Reformları]][[uk:Реформи Тайка]][[vi:Cải cách Taika]][[zh-classical:大化革新]][[zh-min-nan:Tāi-hoà Kái-sin]][[zh-yue:大化革新]][[zh:大化改新]]
{{short description|Imposition of Chinese culture and society on Japan by Emperor Kōtoku, starting in 645}}
The '''{{nihongo|Taika Reforms|大化の改新|"Taika no Kaishin|Reformation of Taika"}}''' were a set of doctrines established by [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (孝徳天皇 ''Kōtoku [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]'') in the year 645. They were written shortly after the death of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and the defeat of the [[Soga clan]] (蘇我氏 ''Soga no uji''), uniting {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The reforms also artistically marked the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and the beginning of the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}}.<ref>{{cite book |author=Fred S. Kleiner, Christin J. Mamiya |title=Gardner's art through the ages |publisher=[[Thomson/Wadsworth]] |location=Belmont, CA |year=2005 |pages=222 |isbn=0-534-64095-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Rowthorne | first = Chris | title = Lonely Planet Japan | publisher = [[Lonely Planet Publications]] | location = Hawthorn | year = 2003 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 34] | isbn = 1-74059-162-3 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/lonelyplanetjapa00chri/page/34 }}</ref> Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (the future {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}), [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], and Emperor Kōtoku jointly embarked on the details of the Reforms. Emperor Kōtoku then announced the era of "{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}}" (大化), or "Great Reform".
The Reform began with {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform|1=Land reform}}, based on [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] ideas and {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=philosophies|1=Chinese philosophy}} from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}, but the true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn seemingly everything from the {{ill|Written Chinese|en|Written Chinese|WD=Q3110592|lt=Chinese writing system|1=Written Chinese}}, {{ill|Chinese literature|en|Chinese literature|WD=Q456012|lt=literature|1=Chinese literature}}, {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion|1=Religion}}, and {{ill|Architecture|en|Architecture|WD=Q12271|lt=Architecture|1=Architecture}}, to even dietary habits at this time. Even today, the impact of the reforms can still be seen in contemporary {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese cultural|1=Culture of Japan}} life.
==Background== [[Image:irukaansatsuzu.jpg|thumb|Isshi Incident]] After the regency of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} ended, the [[Soga clan]], from which Shōtoku's ancestry was derived, took hegemony of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}. The clan was opposed to Shōtoku's son [[Yamashiro Ōe]] and killed him in 643. Under the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}} the Soga clan head, {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}, was virtually an almighty leader of the court.
Those who were against Soga's dictatorship included the emperor's brother [[Emperor Kōtoku|Karu]], the emperor's son, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Prince Naka no Ōe|1=Emperor Tenji}}, along with his friend {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}, and his son-in-law [[Soga no Ishikawamaro]] (Iruka's cousin). They ended Iruka's regime by a coup d'état in 645 (''{{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|WD=Q1062771|lt=Isshi incident|1=Isshi incident}}''). As Kōgyoku renounced her throne, Karu ascended to be [[Emperor Kōtoku]].
The new emperor, together with the Imperial Prince [[Naka no Ōe]], issued a series of reform measures that culminated in the Taika Reform Edicts in 646. At this time, two scholars, {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamuko no Kuromaro|1=Takamuko no Kuromaro}} and priest Min (who had both accompanied {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko|1=Ono no Imoko}} in travels to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui China|1=Sui dynasty}}, where they stayed for more than a decade), were assigned to the position of ''[[Kuni no Hakase]]'' (国博士; National doctorate). They were likely to take a major part in compiling these edicts which in essence founded the Japanese imperial system and government.
The ruler according to these edicts was no longer a clan leader but {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} (in Japanese, ''[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]''), who exercised absolute authority. The reform was much inspired by the {{ill|Japanese missions to Imperial China|en|Japanese missions to Imperial China|WD=Q15281711|lt=Japanese missions to China|1=Japanese missions to Imperial China}} in the Sui and Tang dynasties, and sought to {{ill|Sinicization|en|Sinicization|WD=Q1258641|lt=emulate China's system of centralized imperial control|1=Sinicization}} and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}.<ref name=":0">Hane, Mikiso; Perez, Louis G. (2014). Premodern Japan: a Historical Survey. (Second edition ed.). Boulder, CO. {{ISBN|978-0-8133-4970-1}}.</ref>
== Reform == From today's vantage point, the Taika Reform is seen as a coherent system in which a great many inherently dissonant factors have been harmonized, but the changes unfolded in a series of successive steps over the course of many years.<ref>Asakawa, K. (1963). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in Reform of 645'', p. 267.</ref> The major objective was to reassert imperial authority through reorganizing the government, including abolishing the ''[[kabane]]'' hereditary title system previously in place.<ref name=":0" /> A major reform on the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|WD=Q951841|lt=law|1=Law of Japan}} include the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō (Great Law) Code|1=Taihō Code}} written in 702, consisting of criminal and administrative laws modeled after Tang China, leading to the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system.<ref name=":0" /> New offices created include that of the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=''Daijō daijin''|1=Daijō-daijin}} (chancellor), who presided over the [[Daijō-kan|Dajōkan]] (Grand Council of State), which included the {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}}, eight central government ministries, and a prestigious [[Department of Divinities|Ministry of Deities]].<ref name=":0" />
Locally, the country was reorganized into 66 {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=imperial provinces|1=Provinces of Japan}} and 592 counties, with {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=appointed governors|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name=":0" /> Subjects were to be surveyed, land was to be nationalized and redistributed, and private weapons were to be stored in government armories.<ref name=":0" /> A land tax, military service and labor obligations were instituted on subjects.<ref name=":0" /> A permanent capital was also established in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, emulating the grid system of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}'s capital {{ill|Xi'an|en|Xi'an|WD=Q5826|lt=Xi'an|1=Xi'an}}.<ref name=":0" /> An exam system was also established, although unlike in China, it was open only to those from noble families.<ref name=":0" /> The role of women became much more restricted in Japan, especially in official domains, in emulation of China.<ref name=":0" />
The Reform Edicts severely curtailed the independence of regional officials and constituted the imperial court as a place of appeal and complaint about the people. In addition, the last edicts attempted to end certain social practices, in order to bring Japanese society more in line with Chinese social practices. A legal code was enacted, with a reformed bureaucracy and law.<ref name=":0" /> Nonetheless, powerful clans continue to exercise power in the imperial court and in regional governments.<ref name=":0" /> It would take centuries for the conceptual idea of the Chinese-style emperor to take root in Japan.<ref>Batten, Bruce. "Foreign Threat and Domestic Reform: The Emergence of the Ritsuryo State," Monumenta Nipponica, Vol. 41, No. 2 (Summer, 1986), pp. 199-219.</ref> Chinese civilization, including its art, philosophy, literature and architecture, would become much more entrenched in Japan compared to China's system of government.<ref name=":0" />
==See also== * [[Shōen]]—the form of Japanese fiefdom that developed after the Taika Reforms.

[https://books.google.com/books?id=K1MuAAAAYAAJ&q=The+Early+Institutional+Life+of+Japan. ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|WD=Q844822|lt=Shueisha|1=Shueisha}}.
